text: The robotic positioning and strategy can be seen in the photographs of this particular patient.
The setup for right upper lobe S3 segmentectomy shows good visualization.
It is important to note that you should place your ports well below the area where you intend to operate so that you have clearance and articulation of your instrumentation.

On the left you can see the image without telestration and on the right you can see the image with telestration, demarcating in yellow the phrenic nerve and in blue the outline of the right superior pulmonary vein.

On the left, you can see the separation of the different nodules with the right upper lobe at the top, the head of the patient at the top, the foot of the patient at the bottom, and the right middle lobe to the right in the middle, and the right lower lobe to the bottom on the left.
The right upper lobe S3 segmentectomy began with the first step of identification of critical portions of the surgery.
The phrenic nerve should always be avoided with electrocautery or instrumentation and stapling.
We began by dissecting into the anterior hilum to evaluate the right superior pulmonary vein and encircling, stapling, and dividing the S3 segmentectomy, the V3 vein for the S3 segmentectomy.
At the beginning of the procedure, the ports are placed with camera visualization and are adjusted such that they are barely inside the chest.
We utilize these small sponges to absorb blood rather than the cigars that are traditionally shown for robotic surgery.
By taking down the pleura and using a Cartier, we are able to pass behind the right superior anterior branch of the vein of the V
branch, this right upper lobe S3 segmentectomy allows us to encircle, staple, and divide the right upper lobe anterior segmental vein.
By encircling, stapling, and dividing this vein first, we can then utilize retraction of the lung to evaluate the artery.
The bronchus should lie just posterior to the artery.
It is important to make sure that only the vein draining the anterior segment of the right upper lobe is taken.
The posterior vein will be seen going deep and posterior and the more superior vein will be seen as a separate branch off that lower vein typically.
Dissecting out the superior vein is necessary to make sure that you do not inadvertently remove venous drainage from one of the other segments.
In cases where the venous drainage is questionable, you should dissect further and further out until you are sure which segments are being drained.
We now begin to dissect out the truncus anterior branch of the right upper lobe pulmonary artery.
The right upper lobe anterior segmental pulmonary artery, also off the truncus, can be seen with all the lymph nodes being taken as we encounter them at each branch point.

By being careful to take that first branch of the pulmonary artery going to the right upper lobe anterior segment, we can encircle it with a Cartier and use bovie electrocautery or bipolar to take any lymph nodes in the area.

It is important to try to encircle the artery from both directions, making sure you can pass from the bottom as well as the top to make sure that you do not inadvertently cause damage from pushing too hard when there's tissue from the posterior aspect of the right upper lobe pulmonary artery that might be tethering it posteriorly, making it difficult to pass a stapler.
As you can see, the device is easily passed posterior to the right upper lobe anterior segmental pulmonary artery after quite a bit of dissection, first from the top and then from the bottom.

Using the small sponge to absorb any blood in the field helps with visualization as blood flooding the field or accumulating in the field will often absorb most, if not all, of the light and make visualization quite difficult.
It's important to straighten the instruments and pretend as though you're passing the stapler prior to passing the stapler or clips.
In this circumstance, we felt that we had minimal angle and not enough clearance for passage of a stapler given that there was still some lung parenchyma to be taken.
Therefore, we elected to use a Hemlock Clip Applier on that first branch of the intersegment pulmonary artery.
The Hemlock Clip Applier can easily be used to apply proximally and then distally so that that first branch can be identified and divided sharply with scissors.
Using real-time video, we can show you how quickly it is to achieve hemostasis and achieve division of a segmental branch of the pulmonary artery.

It is quite easy to retract all the tissue in the region and use the hemo-lock to divide both proximally and distally or clip and control both proximal and distal blood flow dividing centrally between the two clips.

However, it's very important to make sure that when performing stapling, the stapler does not catch within the clip, as if it did, it would drag the clip through the staple line and cause tearing and fracturing and injury to the parenchyma with possible prolonged air leak or hemorrhage.
We then inject three to six cc's of ICG to perform firefly visualization showing only perfusion of those segments of the lung that we have taken away by being dark.
Good perfused segments light up green.
Poorly perfused segments are dark.
When an artery has been taken to the segment, the segment that you are resecting is dark.

And when an artery has been taken to the appropriate segment, such as the right upper lobe anterior segmental artery, stapling the intersegmental plane is quite easy and straightforward in this circumstance.
It is important to make sure you look from both sides and create a generous margin, remembering that this tumor was almost at the border.

We wanted to make sure that we went several centimeters posterior to where the tumor was, so in this circumstance, we elected to go into the posterior segment for a few centimeters to obtain at least what would be a margin greater than the size of the tumor and complete resection of the anterior segment.
This was achieved quite easily, making sure that the tumor is almost located just superior to where we are picking up the anterior segment now.

Once we are able to identify that first staple line and the intersegmental plane, placing the second staple line is quite easy, making sure that we are placing it deep to the hilar structures that we have already encircled, stapled, and divided.

We now place a third stapler into that region, placing it below where all of our hemlock clips are and actually in between the hemlock clips, making sure that we take those hemlock clips that are incorporated in the segment that's to be resected and leaving the hemlock clips that are attached to the remaining pulmonary artery branch that is contingent and connected to the main right pulmonary artery.

After firing the residual staple line of the third staple, which was a green load robotic stapler with robotic controlling and firing, you can now see that we have easily started to complete the segmentectomy.
The bronchus is also taken by encircling, stapling, and dividing the bronchus separately.

Once we have completed identification of the bronchus, encircled, stapled and divided it, we begin by placing our attention towards encircling, stapling and dividing the remaining portions of the right upper lobe anterior segment.
In circling, stapling, and dividing, the remaining portions of that area can be performed by injecting additional portions of ICG up to 3 to 6 cc's into the patient with 10 cc IV flush.
This allows us to easily identify the remaining intersegmental plane.
We have now demonstrated clearly that the right upper lobe superior pulmonary vein branches draining the anterior segment have been separately encircled, stapled, and divided.
The right upper lobe truncus anterior branch going out and supplying the right upper lobe anterior segment pulmonary artery is now taken.
The bronchus has been clipped and divided as well.
And we are now stapling the remaining portion of the parenchyma that separates the right upper lobe anterior segment from the apical segment.
Stapling this intersegmental plane is the final portion of surgery that needs to occur before the anterior segment is separated from the remaining lung.
After encircling, stapling, and dividing all of the hilar structures and then taking the parenchyma
at the intersegmental plane, it is important to make sure that the surgeon always holds close the hilar structures and then applies the stapler around those hilar structures.
By holding on to the hilar structures, you ensure that the stapler is completely removing those structures that need to be removed and saving those structures that need to remain.
This final stapling must make sure that you do not accidentally and inadvertently staple off the phrenic nerve or a portion of the superior vena cava.
It is always important to observe and visualize both the line of the stapler as well as the exit of the stapler and the posterior aspect of where your stapler lies while it is being fired.
The right upper lobe anterior segment is now complete.
We can now begin to place that segment into an endocatch bag.
Although a variety of endocatch bags can be utilized, it is wise to use one that snares that and seals as it is being extracted for ease of use.
We then complete the mediastinal lymph node dissection, taking attention to dissect out the station IV-R lymph nodes.
We label them and name them as they are extracted and sent for the pathologic review.
We then retract the lung anteriorly and encircle the hilum of the lung, retracting it anteriorly and dissect out the station VII-R.
eight and nine lymph nodes.
Starting at the top where the carina is and extracting all the station seven lymph nodes is a critical portion of the staging of the mediastinum for this patient.
Surgeons sometimes elect to resect this portion of the lymph node either at the beginning or the end of the case.
In this particular case, we did it at the end.
